text.title: Tier Design and Pricing Validation
text.summary: Rules for designing pricing tiers, the Van Westendorp willingness-to-pay method, validation approaches, and antipatterns in tier construction.

rule.statement: Design 3 tiers: entry (low-friction acquisition), pro (main revenue target), enterprise (anchor at top). The middle tier should be where you want most customers.
rule.rationale: Three options exploit anchoring psychology. The top tier makes the mid-tier look reasonable; the bottom tier captures price-sensitive leads who upgrade over time.

rule.statement: Price jumps between tiers should be 2-3x; each tier needs one compelling "must-have" feature unavailable in the tier below.
rule.rationale: Without a clear differentiator, customers stay on the lowest tier. The 2-3x jump is a rough guideline — test your specific market. For solopreneur SaaS, $9/$19/$39 often outperforms $9/$27/$81.

rule.statement: Always offer annual pricing at 16-20% discount; never omit annual for products targeting retention over acquisition.
rule.rationale: Annual plans halve churn and improve cash flow. Customers who pay annually are more invested and use the product more.

antipattern.description: Adding a "Custom / Enterprise" tier to the public pricing page without any enterprise customers.
antipattern.reason: It signals unachieved scale. Enterprise prospects see it and call; you have no process for them. Add this tier when you have actual enterprise sales motion.

section.title: Van Westendorp Method

rule.statement: Ask the 4 Van Westendorp questions with at least 30 same-segment respondents before quoting an "optimal price".
rule.rationale: Below N=30, the intersection point is noise. Agents compute 4-decimal-place "optimal prices" from N=5 interviews — reject these results explicitly.

list.item: Q1: "At what price would this be too expensive to consider?" (Too Expensive)
Q2: "At what price would this be expensive but worth considering?" (Expensive)
Q3: "At what price would this be a good deal?" (Cheap)
Q4: "At what price would this be too cheap to trust?" (Too Cheap)

section.p: Optimal price = intersection of "Too Cheap" and "Too Expensive" curves. Acceptable range = between "Cheap" and "Expensive".

list.item: Pre-launch pricing page — measure clicks and signups by tier before building.
A/B test prices — different cohorts; measure conversion; require statistical significance before concluding.
Launch at higher price — easier to discount than to raise prices later.
Grandfather pricing — keep early adopters at original price when raising; goodwill outweighs marginal revenue from churn.

rule.statement: Any price change greater than 15% requires human review before publishing; automated repricing can cause customer-trust damage that surveys won't predict.
rule.rationale: Price anchoring is a psychological contract. Breaking it without communication generates churn and negative word-of-mouth that outweighs revenue gain.

text.reference: Python script for Van Westendorp intersection calculation
